headline: Healthcare and Consumer Lead a Split Session as Semiconductors Crater
body_html:
Thursday's abbreviated pre-holiday session ended with a clear two-speed market: defensive and consumer-facing names climbed steadily while the semiconductor complex suffered its worst collective day in recent memory. Of the 198 names tracked, 109 closed higher against 89 decliners — a respectable advance-decline tally that masked the violence concentrated in chips. Healthcare led all sectors with a gain of 3.17%, followed by Consumer at 1.91%, suggesting investors gravitating toward earnings visibility ahead of the long weekend.

The rotation away from semiconductors was emphatic. The group shed 6.5% as a sector, dragged by steep double-digit losses across memory, test equipment, packaging, and fab-materials names. Sandisk fell 14.1%, Teradyne dropped 13.6%, Amkor slid 12.9%, KLA Corp lost 11.5%, and Entegris declined 11.2%. Corning, while not a pure semiconductor play, tumbled 10.8%, underscoring the breadth of the selloff across technology supply-chain names.

The standout on the upside was financial-data, where Equifax surged 6.1%, S&P Global added 6.0%, and Fair Isaac climbed 5.3% — a clean sweep for the data-analytics cohort. Intuitive Surgical rose 5.9%, anchoring Healthcare's leadership. Apple's 4.8% advance offered a rare bright spot in hardware. With energy flat and industrials and materials in the red, today's tape reads less like a risk-on rally and more like a deliberate repositioning into quality recurring-revenue businesses.

briefs.headline: Financial-Data Trio Dominates the Gainers Board
briefs.body: Equifax, S&P Global, and Fair Isaac each climbed more than five percent, making financial-data the most concentrated pocket of outperformance in the entire universe today. The trio's common thread is durable, subscription-like revenue that investors appear willing to pay up for when macro visibility is uncertain.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Selloff Spreads Across the Entire Supply Chain
briefs.body: Losses ranging from roughly eleven to fourteen percent hit names spanning memory storage, test equipment, advanced packaging, and specialty materials — indicating the pressure was thesis-driven rather than company-specific. The sector's 6.5% aggregate decline was the sharpest among all groups tracked and stood in stark contrast to the broader market's positive breadth.

briefs.headline: Gulf Oil Flows Surge Even as Crude Prices Hold Steady
briefs.body: Record export volumes out of the UAE lifted Gulf oil shipments sharply in June, yet crude prices moved little on Thursday as diplomatic efforts aimed at resolving the Iran conflict appeared to hold. The divergence between rising physical flows and a muted price response suggests traders are weighing supply gains against the prospect of a durable ceasefire.

briefs.headline: Iran Crisis Draws Comparisons to the 1979 Oil Shock
briefs.body: Analysts are revisiting the historic 1979 episode as a reference point for the current disruption, examining how supply interruptions, inflationary pass-through, and demand destruction unfolded over that cycle. Energy equities in today's session finished essentially flat, off just five basis points, implying the market is not yet pricing a repeat of that era's sustained price spiral.

briefs.headline: NATO Summit in Ankara Targets Alliance Unity
briefs.body: Leaders from member states, including senior U.S. representatives, convened in Ankara with a stated goal of reaffirming collective-defence commitments and smoothing tensions that have surfaced within the alliance. A draft summit text circulating ahead of the formal session described the mutual-defence pledge as ironclad, though several structural questions about burden-sharing remain on the agenda.

briefs.headline: Britain's Services Sector Contracts Under Wartime Strain
briefs.body: A closely watched purchasing-managers survey showed the United Kingdom's services industry shrinking at a sharp pace in June, with respondents citing the economic drag from the Iran conflict as a primary factor. The reading adds to evidence that the war's knock-on effects on trade, sentiment, and energy costs are now registering in hard activity data beyond the immediate region.
